title: System Design Interview Questions
subtitle: *HLD • LLD • Scalability • Classic Designs • Backend Focus*

questions.q: Cách tiếp cận một bài System Design trong interview?

blocks.text: Framework 5 bước: Clarify -> Estimate -> Design -> Deep Dive -> Wrap up.

System Design Interview Framework:

Step 1: CLARIFY (5 min)

  - Functional requirements: what does it need to do?

  - Non-functional: scale, latency, consistency, availability

  - Constraints: read-heavy vs write-heavy? Mobile?

  - Out of scope: what NOT to design today

Step 2: CAPACITY ESTIMATION (3 min)

  - DAU (Daily Active Users): e.g., 10M users

  - QPS = DAU * actions_per_day / 86400

  - Storage: messages * size * retention

  - Bandwidth: QPS * avg_payload_size

Step 3: HIGH-LEVEL DESIGN (15 min)

  - Draw components: clients, API layer, services,

    databases, caches, queues, CDN

  - Explain data flow for main use cases

Step 4: DEEP DIVE (15 min)

  - Interviewer picks: DB schema, cache strategy,

    scaling bottleneck, consistency model

Step 5: WRAP UP (2 min)

  - Recap, mention what you'd improve

*💡 Từng bước nói to suy nghĩ của mình. Interviewer muốn nghe tư duy, không chỉ kết quả.*

Scale-out example (Node.js):

  Single server: 1 Node.js process

    -> Bottleneck: CPU-bound tasks, single point of failure

  Scale out:

    Load Balancer (NGINX/ALB)

         |   |   |

      [Node][Node][Node]  <- 3 stateless processes

  Important: Session/state must be EXTERNAL

    Redis: session store, rate limit counters

    Shared DB: persistent data

    Message Queue: async tasks

questions.q: CAP Theorem và BASE vs ACID trong System Design?

blocks.text: CAP: một distributed system chỉ được 2 trong 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance.

rows: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent

blocks.text:
CAP Trade-offs in practice:

  CP (Consistency + Partition):

    MongoDB (default: strong read/write to primary)

    ZooKeeper, HBase

    Trade: unavailable during partition -> returns error

  AP (Availability + Partition):

    Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB

    Trade: may return stale data during partition

  CA (no Partition Tolerance):

    PostgreSQL single-node, MySQL single-node

    Not useful for truly distributed systems

Real world: PACELC extends CAP

  E: even during normal operation, tradeoff Latency vs Consistency

rows: Hệ thống làm gì? User stories, features
Users can post tweets, Like posts, Follow users
Hệ thống hoạt động như thế nào?
Handle 10x traffic spike with no change
Tiếp tục hoạt động khi có lỗi

blocks.text:
Availability calculation:

  99%    = 87.6 hours downtime/year

  99.9%  =  8.76 hours/year

  99.99% =  52 minutes/year  <- most production targets

  99.999% = 5.26 minutes/year

SLA vs SLO vs SLI:

  SLI: actual measurement (p99 latency = 145ms)

  SLO: target (p99 latency < 200ms)

  SLA: contractual commitment with penalty
Load balancer phân phối traffic, tăng availability và scalability. Hoạt động ở Layer 4 (TCP) hoặc Layer 7 (HTTP).
- Health checks: Active (ping every 30s) và Passive (detect failed requests). Remove unhealthy servers tự động.
- L4 vs L7: L4 nhanh hơn (TCP level). L7 thông minh hơn (route by URL, headers, content).

rows: App đọc cache; miss -> đọc DB -> write cache
Cache miss on cold start; stale data
Write cache ngay; write DB async
Nhanh; risk data loss if cache fails
Giảm miss; may cache unused data
App đơn giản hơn; miss penalty

blocks.text:
Cache eviction policies:

  LRU  (Least Recently Used)   - default Redis

  LFU  (Least Frequently Used) - Redis 4.0+

  FIFO (First In First Out)

  TTL  (Time To Live)          - simplest

Cache invalidation strategies:

  1. TTL (time-based expiry)    - eventual consistency

  2. Write-through             - strong consistency

  3. Event-based (pub/sub)     - on data change

  4. Versioned keys            - your drilling system!

Cache stampede prevention:

  - Mutex/lock on cache miss (only 1 fills)

  - Probabilistic early expiry (re-compute before TTL)

  - Background refresh

*💡 Redis data structures: String (simple KV), Hash (object), List (queue/stack), Set (unique), Sorted Set (leaderboard), Bitmap, HyperLogLog (unique count).*
Sharding chia data được thành các partitions (shards) trên nhiều servers để scale horizontally.

rows: KHÓ range queries; resharding tốn kém
Lookup table mapping key to shard
Single point of failure if lookup fails

blocks.text:
Consistent Hashing (hash-based sharding cải thiện):

  Traditional hash: hash(key) % N

    Problem: add/remove server -> rehash ALL keys!

  Consistent Hashing:

    - Hash space = circle (0 to 2^32)

    - Servers placed on circle via hash

    - Key assigned to nearest server clockwise

    - Add server: only neighbor keys migrate (~K/N)

    - Remove server: only that server's keys migrate

  Virtual nodes (vnodes):

    Each server has multiple positions on ring

    Ensures even distribution even with few servers

Used by: Cassandra, DynamoDB, Redis Cluster

rows: Writes đến master; reads từ replicas
Cả 2 có thể write; conflict resolution
Replica confirms trước master ACKs client
Nhanh; risk data loss on failover

questions.q: SQL vs NoSQL — khi nào dùng cái nào?
Message Queues — Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS?

rows: Event streaming, audit log, high throughput

blocks.text:
When to use which:

  Kafka:

    - Need message replay/audit

    - High throughput (IoT, clickstream, logs)

    - Multiple consumers of same data

    - Event sourcing / CQRS

  RabbitMQ:

    - Complex routing logic (exchanges/bindings)

    - Task queues with workers

    - Request-reply (RPC) pattern

    - Guaranteed at-most-once delivery

  SQS:

    - AWS native, zero ops

    - Simple background job processing

    - Auto-scaling with Lambda
Consistent Hashing giải quyết vấn đề của hash-based sharding: khi thêm/xóa node, chỉ một phần nhỏ keys cần redistribute.

Problem with hash(key) % N:

  N=3 servers: key 'user1' -> hash % 3 = 1 (Server 1)

  Add server (N=4): hash % 4 = 2 (Server 2) -> MISS!

  Almost ALL keys remapped -> thundering herd on DB

Consistent Hashing solution:

  Ring: 0 -------- 360 degrees (or 0 to 2^32)

  Servers placed:  S1 at 30, S2 at 120, S3 at 270

  Keys placed:     K1 at 50  -> S2 (nearest clockwise)

                   K2 at 150 -> S3

                   K3 at 300 -> S1

  Add S4 at 200:

    Only keys between S3(120) and S4(200) move to S4

    Everything else unchanged

Virtual nodes: S1 appears at 30, 100, 220

  Provides even distribution with fewer real servers

rows: Fill tokens at rate, each req consumes 1

blocks.text:
// Redis Token Bucket implementation

local key = KEYS[1]

local capacity  = tonumber(ARGV[1])   -- 100 req/min

local refill_rate = tonumber(ARGV[2]) -- tokens per second

local now = tonumber(ARGV[3])

local bucket = redis.call('HMGET', key, 'tokens', 'last_refill')

local tokens    = tonumber(bucket[1]) or capacity

local last_time = tonumber(bucket[2]) or now

-- Add tokens based on elapsed time

local elapsed = now - last_time

tokens = math.min(capacity, tokens + elapsed * refill_rate)

if tokens >= 1 then

  tokens = tokens - 1

  redis.call('HMSET', key, 'tokens', tokens, 'last_refill', now)

  return 1  -- allowed

else

  return 0  -- rate limited

end

CDN Architecture:

  Origin Server (US)           CDN PoPs (Edge)

  [Your API + Assets]          Singapore, Tokyo, Frankfurt...

        |                           |

        +--- CDN Pull ------------> PoP caches assets

                                    |

                              User in Vietnam

                              <50ms to nearest PoP

                              vs 200ms+ to US origin

CDN caching strategy:

  Static assets:  images, JS, CSS, fonts

    Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable

    Filename includes content hash: app.a3b4c5.js

  Dynamic content:

    Cache-Control: no-cache (verify freshness)

    Or: Cache-Control: public, max-age=60

  Cache purge/invalidation:

    - Filename versioning (best)

    - CDN purge API (instant but expensive)

    - Short TTL (simple but wasteful)

Cursor-based vs Offset pagination:

  Offset: GET /posts?offset=10000&limit=20

    Problem: slow on large tables (full scan)

    Problem: data shifts if items added/deleted

  Cursor: GET /posts?cursor=eyJpZCI6MTAwfQ&limit=20

    cursor = base64({ id: lastSeenId })

    Stable, O(1) regardless of position

    Used by: Twitter, Facebook, GitHub API

Index selection strategy:

  1. Most selective column first (high cardinality)

  2. Equality conditions before range conditions

  3. Columns in ORDER BY to avoid filesort

  EXPLAIN ANALYZE query:

  Seq Scan -> consider adding index

  Index Scan -> good

  Bitmap Index Scan -> good for low selectivity

  Watch out:

  - Too many indexes slow down writes

  - Index on low-cardinality columns (gender) not useful

  - Write-heavy tables: minimize indexes

questions.q: Microservices vs Monolith — khi nào chuyển?

rows: Dấu hiệu cần chuyển sang Microservices
Teams độc lập, quá nhiều merge conflicts
Các phần cần scale khác nhau (payment vs catalog)
Product đang ở giai đoạn early/MVP
Deploy cycles chậm vì quá phụ thuộc
Security boundaries rõ ràng giữa domains

blocks.text:
Migration strategy (Strangler Fig Pattern):

  Phase 1: Monolith + Facade (proxy)

  Phase 2: Extract services một từng cái

  Phase 3: Monolith nghiệm trưỜng diên

  Start with the most isolated, least-coupled domain

  Never split a transaction boundary across services

  Rule of thumb: 1 team = 1 service
Event-driven architecture: components giao tiếp qua events thay vì direct calls. Loose coupling, async, scalable.

Event-Driven vs Request-Response:

  Request-Response (tight coupling):

  OrderService.createOrder()

    -> PaymentService.charge() [WAIT]

    -> InventoryService.reserve() [WAIT]

    -> NotificationService.send() [WAIT]

  Problems: slow, cascading failures

  Event-Driven (loose coupling):

  OrderService.createOrder()

    -> publish 'order.created' to Kafka

    -> return 202 Accepted immediately

  PaymentService:     subscribes 'order.created'

  InventoryService:   subscribes 'order.created'

  NotificationService: subscribes 'order.created'

Event Patterns:

  Event Notification:  'something happened' (small payload)

  Event-Carried State: full data in event

  Event Sourcing:      events AS the system of record
Distributed transactions không thể dùng ACID transactions xảy qua nhiều services. Saga pattern giải quyết.

rows: Mỗi service publish event, service khác lắng nghe
Distributed lock, commit hoặc rollback đồng thời
Lưu event và data trong cùng transaction

blocks.text:
Key numbers to memorize:

  Time:

  1 day = 86,400 seconds (~10^5)

  1 month = 2.5M seconds

  1 year = 31.5M seconds

  Storage:

  1 char = 1 byte,  1 tweet = ~300 bytes

  1 photo = ~300KB, 1 video = ~50MB/min

  1TB = 10^12 bytes

  Network:

  1Gbps = 125 MB/s

  Average page = 1-2MB

  QPS estimation example:

  Twitter-like app:

    DAU = 100M users

    Each user: 3 tweets/day, 100 reads/day

    Write QPS = 100M * 3 / 86400 = ~3,500 QPS

    Read  QPS = 100M * 100 / 86400 = ~115,000 QPS

    -> Read-heavy (33x)

  Storage (tweets, 5 years):

    3,500 writes/s * 300 bytes * 86400 * 365 * 5

    = ~1.65 TB/year -> ~8 TB for 5 years

rows: CPU, memory, latency, error rate, throughput

blocks.text:
Four Golden Signals (Google SRE):

  1. Latency:   time to serve a request (p50, p99, p999)

  2. Traffic:   requests per second

  3. Errors:    rate of failed requests (5xx, timeouts)

  4. Saturation: how full is your system? (CPU, queue depth)

  Alerting: alert on SLO breach, not just on symptoms

  Example: alert when p99 latency > 500ms for 5 minutes

           NOT just when CPU > 80%

Requirements:

  - Shorten long URL -> short code (6-8 chars)

  - Redirect short URL to original

  - Scale: 100M URLs, 10M redirects/day

  - URL expiry optional

High-Level Design:

  [Client] -> [API Gateway] -> [URL Service]

                                    |

                          [Redis Cache]  [PostgreSQL]

  [Client] -> [Redirect Service] -> [Redis] -> [302 Redirect]

Database Schema:

  urls { id, code, original_url, created_at, expires_at }

  Index on: code (primary lookup)

Short code generation (7 chars, base62):

  Option 1: UUID -> hash -> take 7 chars (collision risk)

  Option 2: Auto-increment ID -> base62 encode

            ID=1 -> '0000001', ID=1000000 -> '4c92'

  Option 3: Distributed ID (Snowflake) -> base62

API:

  POST /urls { url: 'https://long...' } -> { code: 'abc123' }

  GET  /:code -> 302 Location: original_url

Caching:

  Key: short_code, Value: original_url

  TTL: 24 hours (hot URLs stay in cache)

  Eviction: LRU

*💡 Scale bottleneck: Redis read cache cho redirects. Write path có thể async (queue -**>** batch insert). Shard DB bằng hash(code).*

Requirements:

  - put(key, value), get(key), delete(key)

  - Persistent (survive restart)

  - Scale to TB of data

Core components:

  [Client] -> [API Layer] -> [Storage Engine]

                                    |

                     [WAL (Write-Ahead Log)]

                     [SSTable / MemTable]

                     [Bloom Filter]

                     [Compaction Process]

LSM Tree (Log-Structured Merge):

  Write: -> MemTable (in-memory sorted) -> WAL

  When MemTable full: flush to SSTable (disk)

  Read: Check MemTable -> Bloom filter -> SSTables (L0->L1->L2)

  Compaction: merge SSTables, remove tombstones

  Used by: RocksDB, LevelDB, Cassandra

  Write-optimized (vs B-Tree which is read-optimized)

Replication:

  Leader-based: single leader, multiple followers

  Consensus: Raft protocol (leader election, log replication)

Requirements:

  - Limit: 100 requests/minute per user

  - Distributed (multiple API servers)

  - Minimal latency impact

Design:

  [Client] -> [API Gateway / Middleware]

                     |

              [Rate Limit Check] -> Redis

                     |

              [Allow / Reject 429]

Middleware flow:

  1. Extract user_id from JWT

  2. Check Redis: INCR rate:userId:minute

  3. If count > limit: return 429 + Retry-After header

  4. Else: proceed to handler

Redis script (atomic):

  local count = redis.call('INCR', key)

  if count == 1 then

    redis.call('EXPIRE', key, 60)

  end

  return count

Headers to include:

  X-RateLimit-Limit: 100

  X-RateLimit-Remaining: 45

  X-RateLimit-Reset: 1640000000

  Retry-After: 30  (on 429)

Requirements:

  - Send push, email, SMS notifications

  - 10M users, 1M notifications/day

  - Delivery guarantees, deduplication

High-Level Design:

  [Trigger Service] -> Kafka (notification.requested)

                            |

                    [Notification Workers]

                    |           |         |

              [Push Service]  [Email]  [SMS]

              (FCM/APNS)      (SES)   (Twilio)

  Persistence:

    notifications { id, user_id, type, content, status, created_at }

    Index: (user_id, created_at) for inbox

Key challenges:

  1. Fan-out: 1 event -> N users (social notification)

     Solution: fan-out at write (precompute per user) for small fan-out

               fan-out at read (compute on request) for large fan-out

  2. Deduplication:

     Idempotency key = hash(event_id + user_id + type)

     Redis SET NX with TTL

  3. Delivery status:

     Push: FCM/APNs return delivery status

     Email: webhooks (opened, bounced)

     Retry with exponential backoff

Requirements:

  - 1:1 and group chat

  - Message delivery guarantee

  - Presence (online/offline)

  - 1M DAU, 100M messages/day

High-Level Design:

  [Client App] <---- WebSocket ----> [Chat Server]

                                           |

                                    [Message Queue]

                                           |

                              [Message DB (Cassandra)]

                                           |

                                    [Push Service]

                                    (for offline users)

Message Schema (Cassandra):

  messages { message_id (TimeUUID), chat_id, sender_id,

             content, type, created_at }

  Partition key: chat_id

  Clustering key: created_at DESC (newest first)

Presence Service:

  Online: maintain WebSocket connection

  Heartbeat: every 30s, update Redis SETEX user:uid:online 35

  Offline: TTL expires -> offline

Message delivery:

  1. Sender -> Chat Server -> Kafka

  2. Chat Server reads from Kafka, routes to recipient's WS

  3. Recipient ACKs; if offline -> push notification

  4. Message stored in Cassandra (async)

Requirements:

  - Users see posts from people they follow

  - Ranked (not just chronological)

  - 1M DAU, 10M posts/day

Two Approaches:

  Fan-out on Write (Push Model):

    When user A posts -> immediately push to all followers' feeds

    Redis: ZADD feed:userId timestamp postId

    Read: O(1) -> ZREVRANGE feed:userId 0 19

    Problem: celebrity problem (10M followers -> 10M writes)

  Fan-out on Read (Pull Model):

    When user reads feed -> query all followees' recent posts

    Merge and sort by timestamp

    Problem: slow for users who follow many people

  Hybrid (most production systems):

    Normal users (<1K followers): fan-out on write

    Celebrities (>1K followers):  fan-out on read

  Redis data model:

    feed:{userId} -> Sorted Set (score=timestamp, member=postId)

    post:{postId} -> Hash (content, author, likes, created_at)

*💡 Twitter sử dụng hybrid approach. Instagram dùng fan-out on write. Facebook dùng đồng thời cả hai.*

Requirements:

  - Top 5 suggestions as user types

  - < 100ms latency

  - 10M queries/day

Approach 1: Trie (prefix tree):

  'ca' -> [car, card, cat, cafe, care]

  Works well for small datasets

Approach 2: Redis (production):

  Offline job: count query frequency, build sorted sets

  ZADD suggest:ca 1000 car

  ZADD suggest:ca 800  card

  ...

  Query: ZREVRANGEBYSCORE suggest:${prefix} +inf -inf LIMIT 0 5

  -> O(log N + k)

Approach 3: Elasticsearch:

  Completion suggester field type

  Phrase suggester for typo tolerance

Pipeline:

  1. Log all search queries

  2. Batch job (hourly): count frequency

  3. Update Redis/Elasticsearch with top suggestions

  4. API: GET /autocomplete?q=ca -> ['car','card','cat']

Caching:

  Cache responses for popular prefixes (ca, car, app...)

Requirements:

  - Match rider with nearby driver

  - Real-time location tracking

  - Price estimation, ETA

  - 1M rides/day

Components:

  [Rider App] <-> [WebSocket Gateway] <-> [Driver App]

                          |

                  [Location Service] -> Redis Geo

                          |

                  [Matching Service]

                          |

                  [Pricing Service]

Location tracking:

  Drivers send GPS every 5 seconds

  Redis: GEOADD drivers lng lat driverId

  Find nearby: GEORADIUS drivers 106.66 10.77 5 km

              ASC COUNT 10

Matching algorithm:

  1. Find drivers within 5km radius

  2. Filter by: available status, rating

  3. Rank by: distance, ETA

  4. Offer to driver (30s timeout)

  5. If declined: next driver

Real-time updates:

  WebSocket: driver location updates to rider

  Rider app: receives ETA updates every 5s

Pricing (surge):

  Demand/Supply ratio per hexagon (H3 library)

  surge_multiplier = f(demand, supply, time)

Requirements:

  - Upload videos, stream on-demand

  - 500 hours uploaded/minute

  - Adaptive bitrate streaming

Upload pipeline:

  [Client] --(large file)--> [Upload Service]

                                    |

                         [Raw Video Storage (S3)]

                                    |

                         [Video Processing Queue]

                                    |

                  [Transcoding Workers (FFmpeg)]

                  480p, 720p, 1080p, 4K

                                    |

                  [Processed Video Storage (S3/CDN)]

Streaming:

  HLS (HTTP Live Streaming):

    video.m3u8 (playlist) -> segment_001.ts, segment_002.ts...

    Player requests next segment based on bandwidth

    CDN serves segments from nearest PoP

Adaptive Bitrate (ABR):

  Player monitors download speed

  Switches quality: if slow bandwidth -> 480p

                    if fast bandwidth -> 1080p

Metadata DB (PostgreSQL):

  videos { id, title, user_id, duration, views, status }

  Index: (user_id, created_at), (views DESC)

Recommendation:

  Collaborative filtering (ML model)

  Served from separate recommendation service

Requirements:

  - sub-millisecond reads

  - 1TB total cache size

  - High availability

Architecture:

  [Client] -> [Cache Client Library]

                    |

          [Consistent Hashing Ring]

          /         |          \

  [Cache Node 1] [Node 2] [Node 3]

  (primary)     (primary) (primary)

       |              |          |

  [Replica 1]   [Replica 2] [Replica 3]

  Client knows topology -> hashes key -> finds node

  Replication: async, master-replica per node

Data model:

  - Key-value with TTL

  - Data structures: String, Hash, List, Set, ZSet

Cache partition strategy:

  7 GB per shard (Redis single-threaded perf peak)

  Total 1TB / 7GB = ~143 shards

  With 3x replication: ~429 Redis instances

Failure handling:

  Primary fail: replica promoted (sentinel/cluster)

  Split-brain: Raft consensus for leader election

Memory optimization:

  Ziplist encoding for small hashes/lists

  Integer encoding for numeric values

Requirements:

  - Process payments reliably

  - Exactly-once semantics

  - Fraud detection

High-Level Design:

  [Client] -> [API Gateway] -> [Payment Service]

                                     |

                         [Idempotency Check] -> Redis/DB

                                     |

                         [Payment Processor] (Stripe/PayPal)

                                     |

                         [Transaction DB (PostgreSQL)]

                                     |

                         [Event Bus] -> [Notification, Ledger]

Exactly-once payment:

  1. Generate idempotency_key = UUID (client-side)

  2. Include in header: Idempotency-Key: uuid

  3. Server: check Redis/DB for this key

     If exists: return cached response (no re-charge)

     If new: process, store key + result

Double-entry bookkeeping:

  debit_entry  { account_id: buyer,  amount: -100, txn_id }

  credit_entry { account_id: seller, amount: +100, txn_id }

  Always balanced: sum of all entries = 0

Fraud detection:

  Rules engine: velocity checks, unusual amounts

  ML model: anomaly detection (async, don't block payment)

Requirements:

  - Upload/download files

  - Sync across devices

  - Share files/folders

  - 50M users, avg 200GB/user

Architecture:

  [Client (Desktop/Mobile)]

    |

    v

  [Upload API] -> [Chunk Splitter]

  Each file: split into 4MB chunks

  Dedup: hash(chunk) -> already stored? skip

  Storage:

    Metadata:  PostgreSQL (files, folders, versions)

    Chunks:    S3 / HDFS (content-addressed storage)

    CDN:       for popular files

  Sync protocol:

    Long polling or WebSocket for change notifications

    Client maintains local Merkle tree

    On change: diff -> upload only changed chunks

Conflict resolution:

  Dropbox approach: keep both versions, user resolves

  Google Drive: operational transforms (real-time)

Schema:

  files { id, name, owner_id, size, content_hash }

  chunks { file_id, chunk_index, chunk_hash, s3_path }

  shares { file_id, shared_with, permission }

Requirements:

  - Ingest millions of metrics per second

  - Query: sum, avg, p99 over time ranges

  - Alerting on threshold breaches

Architecture:

  [Services] -> StatsD/OpenTelemetry

                    |

  [Collectors: Prometheus / VictoriaMetrics]

                    |

  [Time-Series DB: InfluxDB / TimescaleDB]

                    |

  [Query Engine]  [Alert Manager]

                    |

             [Grafana Dashboard]

Data model (time-series):

  {

    metric: 'api.latency.p99'

    tags: { service: 'order-api', region: 'us-east' }

    timestamp: 1640000000

    value: 145.3

  }

Storage optimization:

  Delta encoding: store diffs instead of absolute values

  Run-length encoding: repeated values compressed

  Rollup: 10s precision -> 1min -> 1hour (data retention)

Cardinality:

  High cardinality (too many unique tag combinations)

  = storage explosion

  Don't use: user_id, session_id as tags

Requirements:

  - Schedule jobs: immediate, delayed, recurring (cron)

  - Exactly-once execution

  - Millions of jobs/day

Architecture:

  [API] -> [Job DB (PostgreSQL)]

                    |

  [Scheduler] -> [Worker Queue (Redis/Kafka)]

                    |

         [Workers] [Workers] [Workers]

Job lifecycle:

  PENDING -> RUNNING -> COMPLETED / FAILED

Delayed job:

  Store: execute_at = now + delay

  Poll: SELECT * FROM jobs WHERE execute_at <= NOW()

         FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED (no duplicate pickup)

  Horizon: BullMQ, Celery Beat use this pattern

Cron jobs:

  Store: next_run_at computed from cron expression

  On completion: compute next next_run_at

At-least-once vs exactly-once:

  At-least-once: simple (retry on failure, idempotent jobs)

  Exactly-once: distributed lock (Redis SETNX)

              = lock key while job runs

Failure handling:

  Timeout: if job running > max_time, mark failed

  Dead letter queue: after N retries, move to DLQ

  Alerting: DLQ depth > threshold

Requirements:

  - Store locations of entities (users, shops, drivers)

  - Query: find entities within radius

  - Real-time updates

Data structure options:

  1. PostgreSQL + PostGIS:

     SELECT * FROM entities

     WHERE ST_DWithin(location, point, 5000)

     -> Mature, SQL, flexible queries

  2. Redis GEO commands:

     GEOADD:    store lat/lng for entity

     GEORADIUS: find within radius

     GEODIST:   distance between two points

     -> Fast, in-memory, simple

  3. Geohash (manual):

     Encode lat/lng as string prefix

     '9q8y' covers ~40km x 20km box

     Find nearby: same prefix + adjacent prefixes

     -> Works with any KV store

  4. H3 (Uber's hexagonal grid):

     World divided into hexagons at different resolutions

     Find neighbors: built-in adjacency

     Aggregation: sum trips per hex for surge pricing

Choice:

  Real-time tracking (drivers): Redis GEOADD (updates/s)

  Persistent locations (restaurants): PostGIS

  Analytics/heatmaps: H3

Key services:

  [Client] -> [API Gateway]

    |

    +-> [Product Service]  -> [Product DB (PostgreSQL)]

    |                       -> [Search (Elasticsearch)]

    |

    +-> [Order Service]    -> [Order DB (PostgreSQL)]

    |                       -> [Kafka: order.placed]

    |

    +-> [Inventory Service] -> [Inventory DB]

    |                        (subscribe order.placed)

    |

    +-> [Payment Service]   -> [Payment DB]

    |                        (subscribe order.placed)

    |

    +-> [Notification Svc]  (subscribe payment events)

Inventory management:

  Pessimistic locking: SELECT FOR UPDATE (flash sales)

  Optimistic locking: version column (normal flow)

  Redis atomic decrement for high-throughput

Shopping cart:

  Redis Hash: HSET cart:{userId} productId quantity

  TTL: 7 days

  Persist to DB on checkout

Search:

  Elasticsearch: product catalog

  Faceted search: brand, price range, rating

  Relevance: tf-idf + boost by popularity, rating

Requirements:

  - Collect logs from 1000s of services

  - Search and query: 100GB logs/day

  - Retention: 30 days

ELK Stack Architecture:

  [Services] -> [Filebeat/Fluentd] -> [Kafka]

                                        |

                              [Logstash (transform)]

                                        |

                              [Elasticsearch (index)]

                                        |

                              [Kibana (dashboard)]

Structured logging (JSON):

  { level, service, trace_id, user_id, message, duration }

  -> Elasticsearch can index every field

Index strategy:

  Daily indices: logs-2024-01-15

  ILM (Index Lifecycle Management):

    Hot: SSD, 3 days

    Warm: spinning disk, 7 days

    Cold: object storage, 30 days

    Delete: after 30 days

Full-text search vs term query:

  message: 'query' -> full-text (analyzed)

  service: 'api'   -> term (exact match, not analyzed)

TOTP (Time-based OTP) flow:

  Setup:

    1. Server generates secret (base32 random)

    2. Show QR code = URI with secret

    3. User scans with Google Authenticator

    4. Server stores encrypted secret for user

  Verification:

    1. Client reads 6-digit code from app

    2. Client sends: { user_id, code: '123456' }

    3. Server: retrieve user's secret

    4. Server: compute TOTP(secret, current_time_window)

       window = floor(unix_timestamp / 30)

    5. Compare server_code == client_code

    6. Allow +/- 1 window (30s clock drift)

  HOTP (HMAC-based) for SMS:

    code = HMAC-SHA1(secret, counter)

    Truncate to 6 digits

Security considerations:

  - Store secret encrypted (AES-256)

  - Rate limit OTP attempts (3 tries, 15 min lockout)

  - Single-use: track used codes in Redis (TTL 90s)

  - Backup codes: hash-stored, single-use

  - Recovery: identity verification process

Scale: 300M MAU, 500M tweets/day

Key challenges:

  1. Fan-out: Elon Musk -> 100M followers

     -> 100M Redis writes per tweet!

  2. Timeline generation: < 200ms latency

  3. Real-time trends

Solutions:

  Hybrid fan-out:

    Celebrity (>1M followers): fan-out on read

    Regular users: fan-out on write

    Mix: precompute timeline, inject celebrity tweets at read time

  Storage:

    Tweet content: MySQL (sharded by tweet_id)

    Timeline: Redis Sorted Set (score=timestamp)

    Media: CDN + S3

  Search / Trending:

    Elasticsearch: full-text tweet search

    Redis: ZINCRBY trending:hour hashtag count

    Sliding window for real trending

  Architecture:

  Tweet API -> Kafka (tweet.created)

     -> Fan-out service (writes to follower timelines)

     -> Search indexer (updates Elasticsearch)

     -> Trend service (updates Redis trending sets)

Scale: 2B MAU, 100M photos/day

Upload flow:

  [Mobile] -> [Upload API] -> [S3 raw]

                                  |

                          [Processing Queue]

                                  |

                  [Resize: thumbnail, display, HD]

                                  |

                          [CDN distribution]

Photo metadata:

  photos { id, user_id, s3_key, caption,

           location, filter, created_at }

  Index: (user_id, created_at) for profile page

Follow graph:

  users_following { follower_id, followee_id }

  users_followers { user_id, follower_id }

  Both directions indexed

  Graph DB consideration for friend-of-friend queries

Feed generation:

  Pre-generated user timeline in Redis

  Key: timeline:{userId} -> Sorted Set of photo_ids

  Hybrid fan-out (same as Twitter)

Discovery:

  Explore page: popular content by location, hashtags

  ML recommendations: collaborative filtering

  Real-time trending: Redis sorted sets + Kafka

rows: Không có index, lấy nhiều rows
Small outer table + index on inner
Lookup service biết data ở đâu
User thấy được thay đổi của chính mình ngay
Không bao giờ thấy older version sau newer
Related operations theo đúng thứ tự

blocks.text:
CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation):

  Write side (Commands):

    POST /orders { userId, items }

    -> Write model -> Command DB (normalized)

    -> Publish: OrderCreated event

  Read side (Queries):

    GET /orders/user/123

    -> Read model -> Read DB (denormalized, optimized)

    Built from events via projections

  Benefits: optimize read/write independently

  Cost: eventual consistency between models

Event Sourcing:

  Store EVENTS, not current state

  Account:

    AccountOpened    { balance: 0 }

    MoneyDeposited   { amount: 1000 }  -> 1000

    MoneyWithdrawn   { amount: 200 }   -> 800

    MoneyDeposited   { amount: 500 }   -> 1300

  State = replay all events

  Snapshot: store state at point N, replay from there

  Audit log: built-in

  Time travel: rebuild state at any point
Raft là consensus algorithm đảm bảo distributed systems đồng thuận về một giá trị dù có failures.

Raft roles:

  Leader:    receives all writes, replicates to followers

  Follower:  respond to leader; vote in elections

  Candidate: requests votes to become leader

Leader Election:

  1. No heartbeat from leader -> timeout -> Candidate

  2. Candidate increments term, votes for self

  3. Requests votes from others

  4. Majority votes -> Leader

Log Replication:

  Leader receives write:

    1. Append to leader's log

    2. Send AppendEntries to all followers

    3. Wait for majority ACK

    4. Commit entry, reply to client

    5. Notify followers to commit

Safety guarantee:

  At most 1 leader per term

  Leader has all committed entries (election safety)

  Committed entries never overwritten (log matching)

Used by: etcd (Kubernetes), CockroachDB, TiKV

Functions:

  1. Request Routing: /api/users -> User Service

  2. Authentication: verify JWT, OAuth

  3. Rate Limiting: 100 req/min per user

  4. SSL Termination: handle HTTPS, pass HTTP

  5. Request/Response Transform: v1 -> v2 format

  6. Circuit Breaker: protect downstream

  7. Caching: cache GET responses

  8. Logging/Tracing: inject trace ID

  9. Load Balancing: across service instances

BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern:

  One API Gateway per client type:

    mobile-bff:  optimized for mobile (reduced payload)

    web-bff:     full response

    partner-bff: different auth, rate limits

GraphQL as API Gateway:

  Single endpoint /graphql

  Federated: each service has its own schema

  Apollo Federation stitches them together

Common implementations:

  Kong: extensible, plugin ecosystem

  AWS API Gateway: serverless friendly

  NGINX: high performance, config-based

  Traefik: Kubernetes-native, auto-discovery

questions.q: Database Schema Design — Normalization vs Denormalization?

blocks.text:
Normalization (OLTP):

  Remove data duplication

  Update anomalies prevented

  Users:   { user_id, name, email }

  Orders:  { order_id, user_id, created_at }

  Items:   { item_id, order_id, product_id, qty }

  Products:{ product_id, name, price }

  JOIN users, orders, items, products

  -> Correct, maintainable, slow for complex reads

Denormalization (OLAP / Read-heavy):

  Duplicate data for read performance

  order_items { order_id, user_name, user_email,

                product_name, product_price, qty,

                order_created_at }

  -> Single table scan, no joins, fast reads

  -> Update pain: price changes need update everywhere

Hybrid approach (common in real apps):

  Normalize core data (source of truth)

  Denormalized read models (via events/CQRS)

  Cache frequently read complex queries

questions.q: Long Polling vs WebSocket vs SSE vs Polling?

blocks.text:
Database migration with zero downtime (Expand-Contract):

  Phase 1 (Expand): Add new column (nullable)

  ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN phone VARCHAR(20);

  Phase 2 (Migrate): Dual-write to both columns

  Phase 3 (Backfill): fill phone for existing rows

  Phase 4 (Switch): code uses only new column

  Phase 5 (Contract): drop old column

  Each phase is a separate deployment, no downtime

Problem: Microservices don't have static IPs

  -> How does Service A find Service B?

Client-Side Discovery:

  Client queries Service Registry (Consul/Eureka)

  Gets list of healthy instances

  Client load balances itself

Server-Side Discovery:

  Client calls Load Balancer

  LB queries registry -> picks instance

  AWS ALB + Cloud Map

DNS-Based (Kubernetes):

  payment-service.default.svc.cluster.local

  Kubernetes DNS resolves to ClusterIP

  No client changes needed

Health checks:

  Active:  registry pings /health endpoint

  Passive: detect failed requests

  TTL:     service re-registers every 30s

10 numbers to memorize:

  Users:

  Twitter: 300M MAU

  Facebook: 2.9B MAU

  YouTube: 2B MAU

  Traffic patterns:

  Read/Write ratio typical: 100:1

  Peak traffic: 3-5x average

  Storage:

  1 tweet: 300 bytes

  1 photo: 300 KB

  1 minute video: 50 MB

  Compute:

  1 DB server: ~1,000 QPS (simple queries)

  1 Cache server: ~100,000 QPS

  Network: 1 Gbps = 125 MB/s

Example: Design Twitter

  500M tweets/day

  = 500M / 86400 = 5,800 writes/sec

  Peak: 5,800 * 3 = ~17,400 writes/sec

  Storage:

  5,800/s * 300 bytes = 1.7 MB/s

  1 year: 1.7 MB/s * 31.5M = 53 TB/year

rows: OAuth 2.0 + OIDC, MFA, JWT (short-lived), API keys
RBAC/ABAC, principle of least privilege, resource ownership
Encryption at rest (AES-256), encryption in transit (TLS)
Rate limiting, input validation, CORS, CSRF tokens
Secrets management (Vault/AWS SSM), no hardcoded credentials
Audit logs, PII masking, anomaly detection

questions.q: Idempotency đểm đạt trong Distributed Systems?

blocks.text:
Why it matters:

  Network failure: was the request processed or not?

  Retry: must not double-charge, double-send, double-create

Idempotency Key pattern:

  Client generates UUID before first attempt

  Includes in header: Idempotency-Key: abc-123

  Server flow:

    1. Check Redis: key 'abc-123' exists?

    2. Yes -> return cached response (no re-process)

    3. No  -> process request

    4. Store: Redis SET idem:abc-123 {response} EX 86400

Methods by nature:

  GET, HEAD, DELETE, PUT: idempotent by definition

  POST: not idempotent, need explicit key

Database idempotency:

  INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING

  Unique constraint: (user_id, order_id, event_type)

rows: Mỗi service có DB riêng, isolated

blocks.text:
Problem: WebSocket is stateful (connection pinned to server)

  User A connected to Server 1

  User B connected to Server 2

  A sends message to B -> Server 1 doesn't have B's connection!

Solution 1: Sticky Sessions (Session Affinity)

  Load balancer: IP Hash -> same user always to same server

  Problem: uneven load, hard to scale down

Solution 2: Pub/Sub with Redis (recommended):

  Server 1                    Server 2

  A connected  <- Redis Pub/Sub -> B connected

  A sends msg                        |

    |   -> PUBLISH chat:roomXYZ msg

         -> Server 2 SUBSCRIBED to chat:roomXYZ

         -> Server 2 sends to B's WebSocket

  Library: Socket.io + socket.io-redis-adapter

Solution 3: Dedicated message broker

  Kafka/RabbitMQ for routing between servers

  More complex but very scalable

rows: Có clarify requirements, constraints? Biết scope?
Vẽ được architecture trước khi đi vào chi tiết?
Biết các đánh đổi: SQL vs NoSQL, sync vs async?
Nhận ra bottlenecks và giải quyết được không?
Nói to suy nghĩ, proactive, không im lặng quá lâu?
Khi đi vào chi tiết có đú sâu không?

blocks.text: 💡 Câu nói quan trọng: 'It depends' + giải thích depends vào cái gì. Tránh đưa ra solution mà không biết trade-off của nó.
